items.title: Behavioural public policy should take the psychology of poverty into account
items.author: Coralie Chevallier
items.content: Behavioural policies succeed on average but yield highly heterogeneous outcomes. Reviewing the psychology of poverty, this Perspective argues that variability in conformism, present orientation, personal agency and social vigilance alters responses to default options, self-regulation devices, information campaigns and social norms.

items.title: Acute stress impairs decision-making at varying levels of decision complexity
items.author: Carsten Murawski
items.content: This study finds that acute stress alone has a surprisingly limited effect on decision-making, irrespective of the decision’s complexity. However, on decisions with experienced time pressure, acute stress significantly reduced decision quality.

items.title: Social feedback amplifies emotional language in online video live chats
items.author: Sandro Claudio Lera
items.content: Applying a multivariate analysis to quantify how emotions spread in YouTube live chats, this study shows that peer interactions drive emotional expression up to four times more than video content, with positivity spreading faster but negativity lasting longer.

items.title: Laughter regulation in solitary and social contexts varies across emotion regulation strategies
items.author: Anne Schacht
items.content: Using facial EMG and humor ratings, this study shows how suppression, reappraisal, and distraction differentially affect laughter-related expressions of amusement in solitary versus social settings.

items.title: Large-scale community study reveals information sampling drives fairness decisions
items.author: Alan G. Sanfey
items.content: This citizen science, lab-in-the-field study embedded a classic economic task, the Ultimatum Game, in a museum, capturing >18,672 decisions about fairness from volunteer members of the public, revealing that information sampling shapes responses to unfairness.

items.title: A systematic review and multilevel meta-analysis of the relationship between boredom and arousal
items.author: Thomas Goetz
items.content: A multilevel meta-analysis (N = 6570) revealed that boredom is generally linked to lower arousal. However, heterogeneity in effect sizes rather characterized boredom as a variable arousal state and could be explained by a range of moderators.

items.title: There can be more to consciousness research than theory testing
items.author: Wanja Wiese
items.content: Consciousness research has long been dominated by competing grand theories, yet consensus remains elusive. We propose shifting focus toward construct-based, data-driven, and iterative approaches that identify the empirical building blocks of conscious experience and provide a more cumulative, integrative path forward for the field.

items.title: On the role of theories in consciousness science
items.author: Biyu J. He
items.content: Consciousness Science is entering an age of unprecedented opportunity, thanks to recent empirical and theoretical advances, increasing interest in the topic, and technological advances in neuroscience. The role theories will play in a maturing science of consciousness deserves a closer look.
